nodes.title: API Authentication (keys, headers)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's credential-handling convention — every API key it ever uses, for weather, price, or eventually anything else, follows the same never-hard-coded pattern established here.

nodes.title: Consuming REST APIs
nodes.capstoneContribution: The real weather-API-to-structured-record converter — the piece that lets a live API response slot directly into the same ingestion, classification, and storage pipeline built throughout Mission 1 and the SQL skill folder.

nodes.title: HTTP Fundamentals (methods, status codes, requests/responses)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first live network call — replacing a static CSV snapshot with an actual request to a real weather API, landing eventually in the tables designed in the SQL skill folder.

nodes.title: Pagination
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's historical backfill capability — the ability to pull a complete year of price/weather history, not just whatever fits in one response, to properly train the forecasting models coming in Mission 3.

nodes.title: Rate Limiting and Retry Strategies
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's resilient polling client — the difference between a script that works once in a demo and a service that can run continuously against a real, rate-limited API without getting itself banned.

nodes.title: Building a Resilient API Ingestion Client
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual, production-shaped weather and price ingestion client — the single piece of code every later mission's live-data assumption rests on.

nodes.title: Final AI Energy Operating System Delivery (mission-wide capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The complete AI Energy Operating System — the full realization of the mission first scoped in a single conversation about AI and electricity generation, now an actual working system with real, checkable evidence behind every claim it makes.

nodes.title: Carbon/Emissions Optimisation Layer
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's carbon-intensity signal and emissions-aware optimisation layer — the piece that makes 'reduce emissions' a real, quantified objective the integrated system can actually act on, not just a reward term with an arbitrary weight.

nodes.title: Grid Copilot Dashboard
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual Grid Copilot dashboard — the human-facing surface where every forecast, recommendation, and explanation this curriculum built becomes something a real operator could act on.

nodes.title: System-Wide Explainability Layer
nodes.capstoneContribution: The AI Energy OS's unified explainability layer — the single, consistent mechanism every subsystem's recommendation passes through before reaching a human, directly implementing the original Grid Copilot vision's core requirement.

nodes.title: Research Workspace for Hypothesis Testing
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's Research Workspace — a structured environment for testing hypotheses about grid changes safely in simulation, delivering on the original mission's explicit strategy of proving value in simulation before approaching real infrastructure.

nodes.title: Full AI Energy OS System Integration
nodes.capstoneContribution: The actual AI Energy OS codebase — one integrated system where the Digital Twin, forecasters, RL agent, and router all run together and can answer a question that spans more than one of them.

nodes.title: Branches
nodes.capstoneContribution: The branching discipline every future feature of the AI Energy OS (a new forecasting model, a new RL policy) will be built and tested on before it's trusted enough to reach main.

nodes.title: Git Init, Staging, and Commits
nodes.capstoneContribution: The first commit of the Digital Twin's actual version history — the point where 'a folder of Python files' becomes 'a project with a recoverable past.'

nodes.title: Merging
nodes.capstoneContribution: The mechanism by which every validated improvement — a better forecasting model, a safer battery policy — actually lands in the AI Energy OS's trusted main line, rather than staying stranded on an experimental branch forever.

nodes.title: Rebasing
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's history-hygiene discipline — every future contributor's branch history stays legible enough that a reviewer (human or, eventually, the AI Tutor examining your work) can actually follow what happened and why.

nodes.title: Remotes, Push/Pull, and Pull Requests
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's move from 'a local folder of code' to 'a real, remotely hosted, reviewable project' — the point where it becomes something a collaborator, researcher, or future you could actually pick up.

nodes.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts
nodes.capstoneContribution: The discipline that keeps the AI Energy OS's shared logic (thresholds, reward functions, shared utilities) correct even when two lines of work touch the same code at the same time.

nodes.title: Max-Flow / Min-Cut Algorithms
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's capacity-aware routing calculation — answering not just 'what's the cheapest path' but 'how much power can actually reach this substation given real line capacity limits,' the exact gap Dijkstra's algorithm alone leaves open.

nodes.title: Final Grid Routing and Contingency Module (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's complete grid-routing and contingency module — topology, routing, flow, resilience, and adaptive candidate selection combined into the system that directly answers the mission's founding questions with real numbers from your own topology.

nodes.title: Graph Representations and Traversal (BFS/DFS)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's grid topology model — the graph representation every routing, flow, and resilience calculation in this skill folder will be computed against.

nodes.title: Network Resilience and Critical Node/Edge Identification
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's contingency-analysis module — directly implementing the founding 'what if two substations fail' question from the mission's original scoping conversation, using precise graph-structural criteria rather than guesswork.

nodes.title: Combining Reinforcement Learning with Graph-Structured Problems
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's routing-decision layer — the actual convergence point of this mission's two skill folders, where graph algorithms define the feasible action space and reinforcement learning chooses adaptively within it.

nodes.title: Shortest-Path Algorithms (Dijkstra's algorithm)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's minimum-loss routing calculation — directly answering the mission's original example question, 'how do I reduce energy costs by routing more efficiently.'

nodes.title: Building the Final Feature Table (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's Mission 2 deliverable: a single, reproducible, documented feature table combining demand, weather, and price data, engineered and cleaned, ready to train Mission 3's first forecasting model.

nodes.title: Data Cleaning (missing values, duplicates, outliers)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's data-quality gate — the step that stands between raw merged data and anything a model is allowed to train on, catching exactly the kind of corrupted input that produces confidently wrong forecasts.

nodes.title: DataFrames (creation, indexing, selection)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's analysis layer — the DataFrame that sits between 'query the database' and 'feed a model,' where the actual feature engineering for Mission 3 will happen.

nodes.title: Boolean Filtering and GroupBy Aggregation
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's in-memory analysis layer — the same daily-summary and threshold-filtering logic built in SQL, now available directly on a DataFrame for feature engineering.

nodes.title: Merging/Joining DataFrames
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first unified feature table — demand, weather, and price combined into a single, time-aligned DataFrame, the direct input every Mission 3 forecasting model will train on.

nodes.title: Time-Series Operations (datetime index, resample, rolling)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's time-series feature layer — hourly-resampled, rolling-averaged demand and weather data, in the exact shape scikit-learn's forecasting models expect as input in Mission 3.

nodes.title: Upgrading the Digital Twin with Physical and Economic Realism (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's fidelity upgrade — the version of the simulation that actually reflects real transmission physics, balancing constraints, renewable variability, cascading-failure risk, and market pricing, ready to be the actual foundation for the final Mission 8 integration.

nodes.title: Energy Markets and Pricing (wholesale markets, marginal pricing, time-of-use)
nodes.capstoneContribution: A physically and economically grounded price model for the Digital Twin — replacing the flat or simplistically time-varying price assumptions used since Mission 1 with a marginal-pricing model that responds to actual supply/demand/renewable conditions.

nodes.title: Grid Structure (generation, transmission, distribution)
nodes.capstoneContribution: Physically grounded transmission-loss modeling for the Digital Twin's graph edges — replacing invented loss percentages with values derived from real voltage/current/distance relationships.

nodes.title: Grid Stability, N-1 Contingency, and Cascading Failure Mechanics
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's cascading-failure model — extending Mission 5's connectivity-only resilience check with the capacity-overload mechanism that causes most real large-scale blackouts.

nodes.title: Grid Balancing and Frequency
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's balancing-awareness layer — grounding the battery scheduler's role in the real physical requirement it would actually be serving: absorbing second-to-second imbalance, not just shifting cost over hours.

nodes.title: Renewable Generation Variability and Capacity Factor
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's renewable-generation realism layer — grounding the solar/wind features used throughout Missions 3-4 in the actual physical variability those sources exhibit, rather than treating them as smoothly predictable inputs.

nodes.title: Control Flow (if/elif/else)
nodes.capstoneContribution: Digital Twin threshold logic — the first branch of the overload/safety checks the Grid Copilot dashboard will later rely on.

nodes.title: Dictionaries
nodes.capstoneContribution: The structured reading-record format ({'timestamp': ..., 'demand_kw': ..., 'source': ...}) the Digital Twin uses for every ingested data point, and the direct shape Mission 2's API responses will arrive in.

nodes.title: Error Handling (try/except)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's fault-tolerant ingestion loop — one malformed reading logs a warning and gets skipped, instead of taking down the entire pipeline.

nodes.title: Files and I/O (CSV & JSON)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual data-loading layer — reading historical demand/weather CSV exports and JSON API captures from disk into the same structured-record shape used throughout this mission.

nodes.title: Functions
nodes.capstoneContribution: The first reusable function in the Digital Twin's codebase — classify_demand() — called from every place that previously duplicated the threshold check inline.

nodes.title: Lists
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's rolling window of raw sensor readings — the direct precursor to the pandas Series used from Mission 2 onward.

nodes.title: Loops (for/while)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The batch-classification pass over the Digital Twin's rolling reading window — turns the single-reading threshold check into something that runs across a full day, then a full year, of data.

nodes.title: Modules & Packages
nodes.capstoneContribution: The first real package structure for the Digital Twin's codebase — separating ingestion, classification, and reporting into their own modules that the rest of the AI Energy OS imports from.

nodes.title: Basic OOP (Classes)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The SensorReading class — the Digital Twin's first proper object, replacing the plain dictionary used throughout this mission with something that can validate and classify itself.

nodes.title: Sets
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's active-source tracker — knowing which sensors/substations are currently reporting, and comparing coverage between two data feeds (e.g. weather stations vs. demand meters).

nodes.title: Variables
nodes.capstoneContribution: Digital Twin data ingestion — the smallest unit of the weather/consumption/price data pipeline: holding one sensor reading in memory before it is stored, transformed, or forecasted.

nodes.title: Neural Network Architecture (nn.Module, layers, activations)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The actual architecture of the Digital Twin's first neural forecaster — the specific arrangement of layers and activations that gives it more expressive power than the linear regression baseline from Mission 3.

nodes.title: Final Neural Forecasting Model and Honest Comparison (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's honest neural-vs-classical verdict — whichever model wins, this becomes the actual forecasting component feeding Mission 5's optimisation work, chosen on evidence rather than on which mission felt more impressive to build.

nodes.title: Overfitting and Regularization (dropout, weight decay, early stopping)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's overfitting-detection discipline — the validation methodology that keeps the neural forecaster from being deployed based on a training loss number that was quietly lying about real-world performance.

nodes.title: Sequence Models for Time Series (RNN/LSTM)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's sequence-aware forecaster — a model that natively consumes the last several hours of readings as an ordered sequence, rather than needing every lag manually engineered as a separate feature column.

nodes.title: Tensors and Automatic Differentiation
nodes.capstoneContribution: The literal computational foundation the Digital Twin's neural forecasting model will be built on — tensors holding the feature data, and autograd computing exactly how each weight should change to reduce error.

nodes.title: Training Loop (loss functions, backpropagation, optimizers)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual neural network training procedure — the loop that will run repeatedly to fit the demand forecaster's weights to real historical data.

nodes.title: Final Battery/EV Scheduling Agent with Explainability (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual battery/EV scheduling module — the first piece of the AI Energy OS that doesn't just predict or classify, but recommends and explains a decision, feeding directly into the eventual dashboard described in the capstone architecture.

nodes.title: Markov Decision Processes (states, actions, rewards, policies, value functions)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The formal problem definition for the Digital Twin's battery scheduler — state, action, and reward defined precisely enough that an RL algorithm can actually be applied to it in the following lessons.

nodes.title: Policy Gradient RL
nodes.capstoneContribution: An alternative battery-scheduling policy learned via a fundamentally different mechanism than Q-learning — the comparison point for deciding which family of RL algorithm actually suits this specific problem better.

nodes.title: Reward Function Design
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual reward function for battery scheduling — the single most consequential design decision in this entire skill folder, since every algorithm downstream will optimize exactly what this function rewards, nothing more and nothing less.

nodes.title: RL Training Evaluation and Safe Deployment Practices
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's RL safety and evaluation gate — the discipline that stands between a training reward curve going up and any claim that the battery scheduler is actually trustworthy, echoing the Grid Copilot's designed path from decision support to earned autonomy.

nodes.title: Value-Based RL (Q-learning)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first learned (not hand-coded) battery-scheduling policy — a Q-table the agent has built entirely from trial and error against the reward function from the previous lesson.

nodes.title: Naive Baselines and Time-Series Data Leakage
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's baseline-comparison discipline — no forecasting model is considered validated in this mission until it's been shown to beat the naive baseline on a properly time-ordered split, not just to have 'a good score.'

nodes.title: Supervised Learning: Classification
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first overload-risk classifier — predicting whether tomorrow's peak demand will exceed a substation's safe limit, a direct input to the Grid Copilot's early-warning behavior described in the capstone vision.

nodes.title: Building a Validated Demand Forecasting Model (capstone synthesis)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first production-shaped forecasting model — the actual 'Predict' layer deliverable this entire mission has been building toward, ready to feed Mission 4's neural forecasting comparison and Mission 5's optimisation work.

nodes.title: Model Evaluation Metrics and Feature Importance
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's model-evaluation and feature-selection discipline — the standard every forecasting and classification model going forward will be assessed against, and the tool that will guide which features Mission 4's neural models should also receive.

nodes.title: Supervised Learning: Linear Regression
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first predictive model — a linear regression baseline predicting demand_kw from temperature and hour_of_day, the simplest member of the forecasting family this mission builds toward.

nodes.title: Time-Series Cross-Validation (walk-forward validation)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's proper model-validation methodology — the actual standard every forecasting model in this mission, and Mission 4's neural models, will be evaluated against before being considered trustworthy.

nodes.title: Aggregation (GROUP BY, aggregate functions)
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first real summary reporting — average, peak, and total demand per substation per day, the exact shape of report the Grid Copilot dashboard will eventually surface.

nodes.title: Indexing and Query Performance
nodes.capstoneContribution: The performance discipline that keeps the Digital Twin's queries usable at real scale — tens of millions of readings, not the seeded thousands used throughout this skill folder — the difference between a demo and a system that survives production data volumes.

nodes.title: JOIN
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's ability to answer questions that span both readings and metadata — e.g. 'which substations exceeded 90% of their rated capacity today' — which requires combining two tables, not just querying one.

nodes.title: Schema Design for Time-Series Data
nodes.capstoneContribution: The actual table definitions (readings, sources, and soon weather and price tables) the rest of the Digital Twin's SQL layer is built on top of.

nodes.title: SELECT and WHERE
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first real query layer against a proper readings table — replacing 'load the whole CSV and filter in Python' with 'ask the database for exactly what's needed.'

nodes.title: Window Functions
nodes.capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's trend-detection queries — a rolling average alongside every individual reading, the SQL-level version of the smoothing Mission 3's pandas and forecasting work will build on.
